mechanism: Oxidizes bacterial proteins, kills C. acnes, reduces resistance

studies.title: Benzoyl peroxide for acne vulgaris: systematic review
studies.findings: Consistently effective across multiple RCTs; reduces inflammatory and non-inflammatory lesions

notes: First-line acne treatment per AAD guidelines. Start at 2.5% to reduce irritation.

mechanism: Normalizes follicular keratinization, prevents microcomedone formation, anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Efficacy and safety of adapalene gel 0.1% in acne vulgaris: meta-analysis
studies.findings: Significant reduction in lesion counts vs placebo; superior tolerability vs tretinoin

notes: OTC in US. Start 2-3x/week to minimize retinization. Photosensitizing.

mechanism: Lipophilic beta-hydroxy acid penetrates pores, dissolves sebum, anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Salicylic acid as a peeling agent: a comprehensive review
studies.findings: Effective for comedonal and inflammatory acne; safe in concentrations up to 2%

notes: OTC up to 2%. Best for oily, acne-prone skin. Avoid in pregnancy.

mechanism: Antibacterial against C. acnes, anti-inflammatory, normalizes keratinization

studies.title: Azelaic acid 15% gel in the treatment of acne vulgaris
studies.findings: Significant reduction in inflammatory lesions; comparable to benzoyl peroxide 5% and clindamycin 1%

notes: Good for sensitive and darker skin tones (reduces PIH risk). Prescription 15-20%.

mechanism: Regulates sebum production, anti-inflammatory, strengthens barrier

studies.title: Topical niacinamide for acne vulgaris: double-blind study
studies.findings: 4% niacinamide significantly reduced acne lesions; comparable to 1% clindamycin

notes: Very well tolerated. Excellent adjunct to retinoids or BP.

mechanism: First-generation retinoid; increases cell turnover, prevents microcomedones, anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Topical retinoids in acne vulgaris: Cochrane review
studies.findings: Tretinoin significantly reduces comedonal and inflammatory lesions; established standard of care

notes: Prescription only. Gold standard for comedonal acne. High irritation potential; requires titration.

mechanism: Lincosamide antibiotic; bacteriostatic against C. acnes, anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Systematic review of topical antibiotics for acne
studies.findings: Effective as monotherapy; combination with BP reduces resistance risk

notes: Always combine with benzoyl peroxide to prevent antibiotic resistance.

mechanism: Sebum regulation, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, wound healing

studies.title: Zinc therapy in dermatology: a review
studies.findings: Both topical and oral zinc demonstrate anti-acne effects; well tolerated

notes: Gentle option for sensitive skin. Works well with niacinamide.

mechanism: Terpinen-4-ol demonstrates antibacterial and anti-inflammatory activity

studies.title: Tea tree oil gel for mild to moderate acne: randomized controlled trial
studies.findings: 5% tea tree oil significantly improved acne severity; slower onset but comparable efficacy to 5% benzoyl peroxide at 12 weeks

notes: Natural alternative to BP. Slower onset. Can cause contact dermatitis in some individuals.

mechanism: Alpha-hydroxy acid exfoliates surface skin, prevents follicular plugging

studies.title: Glycolic acid peels for acne: clinical trial
studies.findings: Effective for comedonal acne and post-acne hyperpigmentation

notes: Better for surface exfoliation than pore penetration. Use with BHA for best results.

mechanism: Keratolytic, antibacterial, reduces sebum. Converts to pentathionic acid which is toxic to bacteria.

studies.title: Historical use and limited modern RCT data
studies.findings: Used for centuries; clinical data limited compared to BP and retinoids

notes: Often combined with resorcinol. Unpleasant odor. Good for sensitive skin.

mechanism: Keratolytic, antibacterial, reduces sebum. Converts to pentathionic acid which is toxic to bacteria.

studies.title: Historical use and limited modern RCT data
studies.findings: Used for centuries; clinical data limited compared to BP and retinoids

notes: Often combined with resorcinol. Unpleasant odor. Good for sensitive skin.

mechanism: Alpha-hydroxy acid with antibacterial properties; larger molecule = slower penetration = less irritation

studies.title: Limited RCT data for acne specifically
studies.findings: Primarily used for anti-aging; limited acne-specific trials

notes: Good for darker skin tones due to low irritation risk.

mechanism: Retinol-like gene expression (RXR/RAR activation); anti-inflammatory, antibacterial

studies.title: Prospective, randomized, double-blind assessment of topical bakuchiol and retinol
studies.findings: Bakuchiol comparable to retinol for anti-aging; less irritation; some anti-acne benefits via antibacterial activity

notes: Pregnancy-safe retinol alternative. Emerging evidence for acne.

mechanism: Madecassoside and asiaticoside promote wound healing, anti-inflammatory, collagen synthesis

studies.title: Traditional use supported by in vitro and limited clinical data
studies.findings: Strong in vitro evidence; limited acne-specific clinical trials

notes: Excellent for post-acne healing and reducing PIH. Adjunct therapy, not monotherapy.

mechanism: EGCG reduces sebum production, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial

studies.title: Green tea extract and acne: open-label study
studies.findings: 2% green tea lotion reduced sebum production and improved acne in 60 days

notes: Good adjunct. Limited as monotherapy.

mechanism: Humectant at low concentrations; keratolytic at higher concentrations; softens comedones

studies.title: Limited acne-specific trials; primarily used for dry skin conditions
studies.findings: Used in combination products for comedonal acne

notes: Better for dry, acne-prone skin. Adjunct only.

mechanism: Promotes cell proliferation, soothes irritation, wound healing

studies.title: Limited acne-specific clinical data
studies.findings: Well-established soothing properties; limited acne-specific RCTs

notes: Excellent for calming irritation from other acne treatments. Adjunct only.

mechanism: Alpha-hydroxy acid; exfoliates, improves hydration, stimulates cell turnover

studies.title: Lactic acid as a new therapeutic peeling agent in acne
studies.findings: 92% lactic acid peels effective for comedonal and papular acne

notes: Gentler than glycolic acid. Good for sensitive, acne-prone skin.

mechanism: Plasmin inhibitor; reduces inflammation-induced melanin production

studies.title: Topical tranexamic acid for melasma and PIH: systematic review
studies.findings: Effective for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation; limited direct acne lesion data

notes: Primarily for PIH prevention in acne. Not a primary acne treatment.

mechanism: Tannins provide astringent properties; mild anti-inflammatory

studies.title: Limited modern clinical trials for acne
studies.findings: Traditional use; some in vitro antimicrobial data

notes: Often contains alcohol which can irritate. Limited evidence.

mechanism: Balances skin microbiome, reduces C. acnes colonization, strengthens barrier

studies.title: Topical application of probiotics in skin conditions: a review
studies.findings: Promising preliminary data; more RCTs needed for acne-specific recommendations

notes: Emerging area. Limited standardized products. Good adjunct.
